description:
You are logged in as an IT-support technician whose home directory is /home/user.

Background  
The help-desk system exported two CSV files into /home/user/tickets:

1. /home/user/tickets/open_tickets.csv  
2. /home/user/tickets/closed_tickets.csv  

You have to produce three new, **plain-text, TAB-separated** files by using classic column manipulation tools such as “cut” and “paste” (feel free to chain them with other coreutils as needed).  

File specifications the automated grader will check:

A. /home/user/tickets/assignment_matrix.tsv  
   • No header row.  
   • Exactly 3 columns separated by a single TAB (U+0009).  
     Columns, in order: ticket_id, priority, assigned_to.  
   • All OPEN tickets must appear and none may be duplicated.  
   • If “assigned_to” is empty in the source CSV, write the literal word “UNASSIGNED”.  
   • Sort rows primarily by priority (order: High → Medium → Low) and secondarily by ticket_id in ascending ASCII order.  
   • A trailing newline (LF) is required.

B. /home/user/tickets/unassigned_tickets.log  
   • Contains ONLY the ticket_id of each open ticket whose assigned_to field was empty.  
   • One ticket_id per line, no extra spaces, lines terminated with LF.  
   • The order must match the order in which those tickets appear in assignment_matrix.tsv.

C. /home/user/tickets/resolution_summary.tsv  
   • First line is a header exactly:  
     ticket_id<TAB>resolution_time_min<TAB>assigned_to  
   • Followed by all CLOSED tickets, one per line, sorted in ascending numerical order of resolution_time_min (shortest time first).  
   • Exactly 3 TAB-separated columns under the header.  
   • A trailing newline (LF) is required.

The grader will use diff to compare your files byte-for-byte against the expected output, so adhere precisely to the formats above (no extra spaces, tabs, or blank lines).

Hints for yourself (not commands):  
• “cut -d, -f…” is handy for extracting comma-separated columns.  
• “paste” can join parallel column streams produced by separate commands.  
• “sort -k” with a custom key order (High,Medium,Low) will help achieve the required priority ordering.

When finished, list the new files to show they exist, and you’re done.
truth:
Initial directory tree and contents before the agent starts:

/home/user/tickets/  
├── open_tickets.csv  
└── closed_tickets.csv  

open_tickets.csv  (7 lines, including header)  
ticket_id,priority,assigned_to,customer,issue_type,status  
TCK-1001,High,alice,Acme Corp,Network,Open  
TCK-1002,Medium,bob,Globex Inc,Hardware,Open  
TCK-1003,Low,,Initech,Software,Open  
TCK-1004,High,charlie,"A&B Consulting",Network,Open  

closed_tickets.csv  (6 lines, including header)  
ticket_id,resolution_time_min,assigned_to,customer,issue_type,status  
TCK-0901,45,dan,Acme Corp,Network,Closed  
TCK-0902,120,alice,Globex Inc,Hardware,Closed  
TCK-0903,30,alice,Initech,Software,Closed  
TCK-0904,15,bob,Acme Corp,Hardware,Closed  

Expected files after successful task completion:

/home/user/tickets/assignment_matrix.tsv  (4 lines)  
TCK-1001<TAB>High<TAB>alice\n  
TCK-1004<TAB>High<TAB>charlie\n  
TCK-1002<TAB>Medium<TAB>bob\n  
TCK-1003<TAB>Low<TAB>UNASSIGNED\n  

/home/user/tickets/unassigned_tickets.log  (1 line)  
TCK-1003\n  

/home/user/tickets/resolution_summary.tsv  (5 lines)  
ticket_id<TAB>resolution_time_min<TAB>assigned_to\n  
TCK-0904<TAB>15<TAB>bob\n  
TCK-0903<TAB>30<TAB>alice\n  
TCK-0901<TAB>45<TAB>dan\n  
TCK-0902<TAB>120<TAB>alice\n
